## [00:00 - 03:00]

Count KPI is dashboard 3 commerce. So in this video, I'm going to take you through the dashboard that I use as a high level view of how my e-commerce accounts are performing. So this spreadsheet is set up in Google Sheets, is set up in a way that you can download data from Ads Manager, copy and paste it into this spreadsheet, into the raw data here, and have a view of your account at the top level. First question you might have is, well, why can't I just use Ads Manager? Reason is that Ads Manager doesn't give you sales conversion rate, doesn't give you AOV, doesn't give you cart conversion rate, what percent got to your cart, what the cost was to get someone to your cart. And so that's why I prefer to take the data out and analyze that separately. So let's have a quick look at the dashboard itself. The first thing to note is that your account name should go here, and that should be the same account that's in your Ads Manager. And I'll come to that part of it and explain that once I go through the demonstration of how to download your data. The date here is automatically set to today, and the subsequent days are then the 14 days prior. So you don't need to edit anything here. The date will always show today minus those 14 days. The days automatically calculated. And the rest of this data is pulled from the raw data here. So I'll shortly show you how to get data into this format, copy and paste it out of Ads Manager really quickly into here. And here your account name would sit, and that needs to match what's up here as well. So all of these columns here are automatically pulling through your spend, your clicks. So for clicks here, I'm using outbound clicks. You can use link clicks, but outbound is a cleaner way of looking at data. And you've got your purchases, which is based on your pixel event firing, and then also revenue, again, based on your purchase event firing and passing back the revenue number. So these are all fixed. If your currency is different, you can just highlight that column and change it into a different currency, for example. But I would just make sure to keep the data clean, format your decimal places, just to make it easier to analyze. I mean, who needs to see the decimals on large numbers? It doesn't help. So they're just four columns there for reference. However, the kind of meat of this spreadsheet is looking at the key metrics and understanding why you want to be analyzing them. And also why I've set up the color coordination is it is. So first thing, if you hover, click over or hover over these columns, you can see how they're constructed and what the columns actually mean, what data it's pulling in. If you look down the columns, the color is graded on a column by column basis. So using the conditional formatting tool within Google Sheets is automatically looking at the lows, the highs, and the intermediate numbers, and then grading them from green to red.

## [03:00 - 06:01]

So the lowest numbers for this particular column are 3738. So they get shaded green, the highest are 120. So it's using a temperature gauge to give you an idea of how those numbers look. And what I find really powerful is actually when you scan across, you can quickly see where your bad metrics are and maybe what to do in a given day. So bear that in mind, when you're looking at these, the columns are shaded from top to bottom, but it's been designed in a way that you can scan across day by day and look at which metrics are doing well and which are not. Cost per acquisition is a factor of your sales conversion rate and your cost per click. And so I know, for example, I mean, this is just dummy data just to illustrate the point. But for example, on this day, our cost per acquisition is $74. And compared to here, we've got 55. I can quickly see where the problem is. Now conversion rate has dropped, but cost per click has increased quite a lot. And that's resulted in a high CPA. The other metric I'm looking at for E-COM is also return on ad spend, which is this column here. Return on ad spend is a factor of your cost per acquisition and your AOV. So with this, the 90% ROAS with an AOV of $66, for every $74 we're paying for one customer to be acquired, we're generating $66 back. So that's not a great return. So clearly we can see we've got 151% ROAS over this period. So that's our kind of benchmark. We've got some days that are better, some that are worse, but this day in particular is a bad day. And if I scan across, I can see that quite a few things are not as they need to be. So here our AOV is topped over 100, our average is 86. We have a low AOV today. So that's problem number one. Problem number two is our cost per click is higher than the average. And today it's actually the second highest it's been in that period. And our sales conversion rate is not the worst it's been, but it's not great either. So those factors have come together to determine that actually the performance today is down to a few different factors not working so well. ## [06:01 - 09:01]

you need to make sure that your events are firing in the right way. So I've got website content views, as to cart, checkouts initiated, purchases, et cetera, that you can see up here. For some reason, people have not added to cart at the same rate they have been on previous days. So when it comes to analyzing your performance, the first thing you want to look at is how's my cost per acquisition and how's my ROAS? But then breaking it down into the sub parts to look at kind of where the key problems are or where the opportunities are. And for example, in this, if I look at my sales conversion rate, I want to know why that's down. Is it that fewer people have added to cart or is it that fewer people have converted that are in the cart as well? So that's how I kind of use this dashboard to determine performance as well. Then I have my kind of tertiary metrics, which are just there for reference. So my click through rate, my registration conversion rate and my lead conversion rate. So registration and leads, people use it in different ways for different sites. For me, I would use registration for any email collection on the website. And I would use lead for any email collection off of the website. So to give you an example, if I'm running cold traffic campaigns and I'm generating leads through the lead generation objective for cold traffic targeting, let's say I'm giving away a PDF or something to bring people in at the top level, I might track my leads and then I have a cost per lead here as well. And then my registration for this example is people that have come to the website and actually registered where I've maybe given away a 5% discount for giving up the email address. So I can kind of measure that here in my cost per range. So they're kind of my tertiary metrics. They're not really going to influence too much of my decision making unless they're a core part of the program. So if I'm doing big lead gen, big registration, capture and they're down, then I can also look at are they having an impact overall as well? So these five columns are the five that I focus on. So I use a tool called Supermetrics. Within Supermetrics, what you can do is actually pull the data automatically from Facebook and also schedule it to have it updated on a regular basis. So you don't have to do the download from ads manager, copying and pasting the CSV. It is a paid tool, so you have this reference as a way of doing it without paying. If you do want to then enhance it, you can use a tool like Supermetrics to pull that data through. And essentially all you would do, so once you know how Supermetrics works, there'll be an add-on installed Supermetrics, you launch your sidebar, and all you're doing is literally pulling the same data that you've seen from ads manager into these columns and loading that through. So that's another opportunity to just speed up your analysis and pull the data you need a lot quicker. So I wanted to show you the more basic way, so that at least without any additional tools, you can get going straight away.
